entries.name: DJ Swill B
facts: DJ Swill B is a Jersey City DJ and producer active since 2020, known for Jersey club production and collaborations across New Jersey rap.
He is a featured artist on Bandmanrill's 2022 single 'Jiggy in Jersey' (feat. Sha EK), from the album Club Godfather, one of the defining Jersey club releases of that year.
His catalog includes 'My Name' (feat. Mr. Chicken, 2022), 'Roll'n' (2023), 'Down 4 Mine' (2024), and 'COMMITMENT' (2025), available on Apple Music and major platforms.
He appeared in Mr. Chicken's 'Never Could' video (2021), covered by No Jumper, introducing the Jersey City scene to a national audience.
Additional credits include 'Shake Dat' with Asian Doll (2022) and 'Choppa in the Backseat' with Albee Al (2024).

entries.name: Wiztv
facts: Wiztv — formerly billed as DJ Wizard, born John Freeman — is a Jersey City DJ, videographer, and documentarian who has been archiving the city's music scene on video since at least 2006.

He directed and produced 'WizTV Presents: The Jersey City DJ Documentary Vol. 1' (2006), a nearly three-hour document of Jersey City's mixtape DJ scene that remains the primary visual record of that era.
He is the producer of the Jersey Club Blends mixtape series, including Vol. 3 (2017), and created a blend with DJ Sliink titled 'Not Gunna Get Us' that circulated widely on SoundCloud.

He built the WizTV video platforms — long a leading source for Jersey club party and sweet-sixteen footage across New Jersey — and, to preserve and properly credit the culture, has moved his video archive from YouTube to Patreon.
As of 2026, he is producing a new WizTV Jersey Club Documentary, with Part 1 previewed via Patreon and Instagram, continuing his role as the scene's primary visual historian.

Widely recognized as the originator of visual coverage for Jersey club culture, he was the first and most consistent figure to document the scene from the inside — capturing party energy, dancers, DJs, and promoters across New Jersey long before social media or viral dance platforms existed.
As a videographer and director he is regarded as a pioneer to Newark’s Jersey club DJs, whose parties, sets, and battles he filmed and preserved as the movement lived and breathed on dance floors.

entries.name: Mista Quietman
facts: Mista Quietman (Antwan Anderson) is a Jersey City DJ and remixer active since at least 2012, and a key figure in preserving the city's DJ scene history online.

He produced 'The 201 High School Anthem', a Jersey City rework of DJ Sliink's 'High School Anthem', calling out Lincoln, Ferris, Dickinson, and Snyder high schools -- a local anthem of the city's school-era DJ culture.
His SoundCloud catalog dating to 2012 includes Jersey club loops, remixes, and gospel club mixes such as 'The Sharpbounce 2ktwelve' and 'C4 Rock'.

He runs The Real Mista Quietman YouTube channel, where he has preserved the full upload of WizTV's Jersey City DJ Documentary Vol. 1 (2006), making one of the most important documents in Jersey City music history accessible to the public.
He is listed as a supporter in the TTE Edits Bandcamp community, connecting the Jersey City scene to the wider Jersey club editing world.

entries.name: DJ K (Killa K)
facts: DJ K, also known as Killa K, is a Jersey City DJ whose 'Killa K family' received a direct shoutout from DJ E Double (Double Platinum Entertainment) in the WizTV Jersey City DJ Documentary (2006).
This prominent mention places him firmly in the city's late-1990s and 2000s mixtape scene, a highly competitive era where crew affiliations and shoutouts were markers of street credibility.
His identity and affiliation were confirmed by DJ DX (Robert Van Liew) in an oral history recorded for The Jersey City Sound in 2026, anchoring his place in the scene's historical record.
Operating under the Instagram handle @coalitionboy_k, the 'Coalition' in his name signifies his continued affiliation with the tight-knit networks that define Jersey City's DJ culture.

entries.name: DJ BigTime
facts: DJ BigTime is a Jersey City mixtape DJ active from the 1990s through at least 2006, known in the scene for street-focused mixtape music -- a lane he describes on camera in the 2006 documentary, showing his home studio, crates, and full setup.
He originally performed as DJ Quality before taking the name Big Time, a rebranding that marked his transition into a more prominent role in Jersey City's mixtape distribution network.
Mark Cee credited DJ BigTime with getting Mark Cee's final mixtape (around 2000 to 2001) distributed, reflecting the informal but real influence he had over how music moved through the city.
He served as a mentor figure to DJ Dolo, who says he came up looking up to Big Time -- the kind of generational mentorship that defines how DJ skill has always moved through Jersey City.

entries.name: DJ Flash
facts: DJ Flash is a Jersey City DJ from Park Street who is widely credited in the 2006 WizTV documentary as the originator of the city's mixtape scene and was introduced on camera as a legend.
He began DJing at age seven, learning by convincing older neighborhood DJs including Stranger D to let him spin -- a start that placed him at the founding generation of Jersey City hip-hop.

He practiced all five elements of DJing including beat juggling, scratching, and mixing, and describes pre-sampler production built with two turntables and two tape decks, a workflow typical of the late 1970s and early 1980s park jam era.
He was part of a five-DJ juggle-battle circle from his block, crediting peers including Jamaican Jeff and E Rock as early inspirations, and names Wimpy B as his big brother in the craft.

He also credits a generation that includes Stranger D, Fresh Jeff, and Storming Norman (later moving with Wu-Tang's circle), placing the Jersey City scene in direct connection with one of hip-hop's defining crews.
He operates a store in Jersey City referenced on camera, mentions 271 Ocean Avenue, and stepped back from full-time DJing to raise his children while remaining active in the scene.
He also records as DJ Flash Hamilton and works within the New Jersey Mix Kings 201 collective alongside DJ Hazz B and Semaj da DJ.

By his own account, given to this archive in 2026, he co-produced Double XX Posse's debut single "Executive Class" (Mel-O Records, 1990), handling the drums in a session that also drew on DJ Wimpy Bee, DJ Stranger Dee, and the brothers Sugar Chris and Magic.

By his own account, given to this archive in 2026, the 1950s Jersey City R&B star Roy Hamilton was his great-great-grandfather, and the late Jersey City rapper Rigga Mortis was his first cousin; he records as DJ Flash Hamilton.

sources.label: WizTV Presents: The Jersey City DJ Documentary Vol. 1 (2006)

entries.name: DJ Infamous
facts: DJ Infamous is a Jersey City DJ who operates as an integral part of the local hip-hop and club landscape, proudly representing the city's signature 201 area code.
His handle @inf_201 deliberately centers his local identity, firmly distinguishing him from the Atlanta-based DJ Infamous who works in an entirely different commercial market.
Like many stalwarts of the Jersey City scene, his reputation was forged through local performances and community ties rather than mainstream commercial releases.
His inclusion in the archive preserves the footprint of the working DJs who provide the soundtrack to Jersey City's nightlife and neighborhood events.

entries.name: The Jersey City DJ Documentary Vol. 1 (2006)
facts: Directed by DJ Wizard (WizTV), the Jersey City DJ Documentary Vol. 1 is a nearly three-hour (2:57:58) film documenting Jersey City's mixtape DJ scene as it existed in 2006, the most important single document of that era.

The film features interviews and home-studio performances from DJ Flash, Mista Quietman, Mark Cee, Styles 007, Man Money, DJ E Double (Double Platinum Entertainment), DJ BigTime, DJ Dolo, DJ DX, X5, Chameleon (Ka-Million), Semaj da DJ, DJ Madden, Benito, Joe Bananas, cover designer Blaze, and others.

Bonus footage includes the Winterblaze mixtape battle held at the Boys Club (2000), with battle organizer Sean interviewed and DJ Savage among the competitors -- a rare visual record of the city's battle culture.

The film preserves oral history documenting turntable battles at Audubon Park in the late 1970s and early 1980s where the winner took the loser's equipment, and names scene venues including Rendezvous (formerly Ruthie's), the Boys Club, Lollipop, Taste, and Ferris High School.
The documentary names and defines Jersey City style markers including blend-driven mixtapes, the 201 style of doubled drums, and stacked-acapella blends, making it the source record for these terms.

entries.name: Stan's Square Records
facts: Stan's Square Records was a Jersey City record store founded by Stan Krause in 1965 in Journal Square, later moving to 349 Bergen Avenue
For five decades the store was a cornerstone for music lovers and record diggers, known especially for preserving and promoting acappella, soul, and rhythm and blues

The store sold mixtapes to the whole community from the city's top mixtape DJs, including DJ 007, DJ Mad Money, DJ E Double, DJ NEL E NEL, DJ Wimpy B, DJ Big Time, DJ Dyce, DJ Ju-Town, DJ DX, DJ Wizard, DJ Madden, DJ Walley Katt & X5, Semaj da DJ, and many more

Krause founded Catamount Records (1964), the label that launched the recording careers of vocal groups including The Persuasions and 14 Karat Soul; Frank Zappa first heard The Persuasions singing over the phone from the store, where Krause was the group's manager

Krause's Catamount acts reached the national stage: The Persuasions charted on Billboard (Street Corner Symphony, No. 16 R&B and No. 88 pop, 1972), and 14 Karat Soul performed on Saturday Night Live (1983) and drew large audiences in Japan
Krause founded the United in Group Harmony Association (UGHA) and produced music and live vocal-harmony concerts

In 2015, Jersey City Mayor Steven Fulop issued a proclamation for the store's 50th anniversary honoring Krause as 'Stan the Music Man of Jersey City'; in 2016 he received the Jersey City Living Legend Award
Krause died on May 20, 2016, at age 74; the store closed in 2018 following his passing

entries.name: Mark Cee
facts: Mark Cee, also known as Nemo, is a Jersey City DJ and ghost producer who is one of the most influential behind-the-scenes figures in the city's mixtape scene, as documented in the 2006 WizTV documentary.
He is credited with schooling a generation of Jersey City DJs on the MPC, including DJ DX, making him a key link in the chain of music technology knowledge that passed through the scene.
He performed as half of the brother duo Double D alongside his brother Shannon, and his final mixtape (around 2000 to 2001) was distributed with the help of DJ BigTime.

entries.name: DJ 007
facts: DJ 007, born Michael Williams and also known as Styles 007 and Mr. Shut Em Down, and formerly as DJ Worm, is a Jersey City DJ who has operated under all of these aliases across a career of more than three decades, first documented in the 2006 WizTV documentary.
Although he spoke in the 2006 film of plans to retire in 2007, he never stepped away — DJ 007 remains an active Jersey City DJ.
He sold mixtapes through Stan's Square Records alongside the city's top DJs, placing him in the commercial heart of Jersey City's mixtape distribution network.
He is named among the community's favorite Jersey City DJs in the Chill Town documentary, cited alongside DJ Nel E Nel and DJ Mad Money.

entries.name: DJ Mad Money
facts: DJ Mad Money — born James Gadsden, and billed over the years as Mad Money and Man Money — is a Jersey City mixtape DJ active since approximately 1989, one of the longest-running figures in the scene's documented history.
He is the producer of the Street Blends series, including Phase 20 and Phase 21 — tape series spanning decades that reflect the sustained output of a career DJ.

In the 2006 WizTV documentary he recalls the era when a Jersey City mixtape could earn a DJ $2,000 or more, and names Devil Seven as a peer from that period, placing the city's tape economy in historical context.
His classic, high-energy “Jersey City-style” mixes from the Chilltown circuit remain highly regarded, and he is counted among the influential DJs of the city's hip-hop and mixtape community.

entries.name: DJ E Double
facts: DJ E Double, born Eric Pinkney and also known as DJ E-Dub DaGeneral and E Platinum, is a Jersey City DJ celebrated as the Prince of Jersey -- one of the city's most prominent mixtape and club DJs of the late 1990s and early 2000s.
In 1995 he founded his entertainment brand DpEnt. (Double Platinum Entertainment), which became the foundation for his promotions, party hosting, and mixtape distribution across the East Coast.

Through the early 2000s he built a reputation for signature blends, club mixes, and custom tracks defining North Jersey's urban nightlife, with residencies at Rendezvous (formerly Ruthie's), Lollipop, Taste, Brenda's, and Bills.
He is interviewed in the 2006 WizTV documentary, where he gives a shoutout to the Killa K family, documenting the social fabric of the scene.
His daughter performs as DJ She Dog, making him part of a family lineage in the city's DJ tradition.

He was the founder and head of Double Platinum Entertainment. The crew's members included Greenbean, Thunderkatt, Smurfet, DJ Q, DJ DYCE, Deejay Luchionney, DJ Walleykatt, DJ DX (Double X), and Exclusive (R.I.P Jamie).

He is a nephew of Lord Sun Albee Al, founder of the pioneering crew Sweet Slick and Slide, and a cousin of the rapper Albee Al and DJ Thunderkatt — tying him to one of Chilltown's foundational hip-hop families.

entries.name: Kamillion
facts: Kamillion — formerly billed as Chameleon and Ka-Million, and earlier as DJ Solid — is a Jersey City producer and DJ.
He operates Paid In Full Studios and Ful Real Entertainment, a company named in memory of a friend known as Ful — a tribute that reflects the loyalty at the center of his work.
He is credited as a producer across the city's scene and remains active on Instagram as @mixedbykamillion.

entries.name: Semaj da DJ
facts: Semaj da DJ — whose name is James spelled backward — is a Jersey City DJ who has been mixing since 1998, coming up by studying the local DJs around him and mastering the craft of mixing and blending.

He founded his own label, T.O.M. Digital Production — also known as Team of Monstars — a collective of close friends who shared his interest in the business, among them DJ Evil, E Baby, Yoshi, and Shady.

His first mixtape reached stores in early 2000; as his fan base grew he expanded the team and his retail footprint — operating record stores in West New York and Union City and reaching into other cities — and he sold mixtapes through Stan's Square Records alongside the city's top DJs.
That visibility, amplified by the rise of social media, led a local filmmaker to include him in the 2006 Jersey City DJ Documentary.

He later released the Jersey City Mix, which went viral on the strength of a defiant hometown catchphrase — spreading across boomboxes, ringtones, and passing cars to become one of the city's most repeated hooks.

entries.name: DJ Madden
facts: DJ Madden, born Steve Garcia, is a Jersey City DJ, turntablist, and producer who was put on by DJ Wizard and mentored by Mark Cee, placing him in the direct lineage of the city's most influential scene-builders.

He collaborated with DJ DX on a trilogy of studio albums: 'This Is Hip-Hop', 'The Unfortunate Child', and 'Made From Scratch' (2015, Apple Music), marking a formal recording output that sets him apart from many mixtape-only peers.

Beyond their album trilogy, he and DJ DX have released a run of underground hip-hop and rap singles — including “1000 Reasons” and “Keeps Me Going” — pairing classic boom-bap with the reggaeton and Latin-urban energy the pair are known for working into their live sets.
He sold mixtapes through Stan's Square Records alongside the city's top DJs and is active on Instagram as @officialdjmadden.

entries.name: Benito
facts: Benito is a Jersey City producer-DJ who was roughly 11 years into his career at the time of the 2006 WizTV documentary, placing his start around 1995.

He is a practitioner of the 201 style of doubled drums -- the production signature that the documentary names as specific to Jersey City -- making him one of the clearest documented examples of that sound.
He worked alongside Double O, and his interview is one of the most technically specific accounts of Jersey City's production culture in the entire film.

sources.label: WizTV Presents: The Jersey City DJ Documentary Vol. 1 (2006)

card.label: Known for
card.value: 201 style doubled drums; the Jersey City production signature

entries.name: Joe Bananas
facts: Joe Bananas is a Jersey City DJ of Bananas Entertainment and Chemical Records who is interviewed in the 2006 WizTV documentary and noted for working in the chopped and screwed style -- a Southern technique he adapted to the Jersey City mixtape format.
His segment in the documentary is dedicated in memory of Jay Vito (RIP), making it one of the film's most personal moments and reflecting the losses the scene had already absorbed by 2006.

sources.label: WizTV Presents: The Jersey City DJ Documentary Vol. 1 (2006)

entries.name: Blaze (Blaze-In-Arts)
facts: Blaze, working under the brand Blaze-In-Arts, is the graphic designer behind the visual identity of Jersey City's mixtape era, called the scene's cover designer in the 2006 WizTV documentary.
He has designed roughly 1,000 mixtape covers since 2003, each marked by his signature crown logo, making him one of the most prolific visual artists in the city's music history.

sources.label: WizTV Presents: The Jersey City DJ Documentary Vol. 1 (2006)

entries.name: DJ NEL E NEL
facts: DJ NEL E NEL, born Nelson Carty and also known as Mandela and billed as Nel E Nel Blends, is a Jersey City blends mixtape legend, DJ, and producer with credits extending from the local scene to nationally known artists.

His production and blends credits include work with Joe Budden, Ransom, PnB Rock, and N.O.R.E., and per the archive's founding oral history, his resume also includes credits with Kodak Black and the duo Hitchcock and Ransom.

He is said by the scene's oral tradition to have put out a Notorious B.I.G. album ahead of its official release, a claim that awaits a second source but reflects his standing as a first-tier figure in Jersey City's mixtape culture.
By community account he won the Winter Blaze 2000 DJ battle held at the Jersey City Boys and Girls Club, the city's premier battle competition of that era.
He sold mixtapes through Stan's Square Records alongside the city's top DJs and remains active on Bandcamp and Instagram as @nelenelblends.
He is repeatedly named among the community's favorite Jersey City DJs in the Chill Town documentary, cited across generations alongside DJ Wimpy Bee.
He is part of the Presidential Beats production team — the beat-making arm of Orlando Wharton's Presidential — alongside Producer Mayor.

entries.name: DJ DYCE
facts: DJ Dyce is a Jersey City DJ who is likely the figure shouted out as 'Dice' in the 2006 WizTV documentary, one of several names called out by peers during the film's interview segments.
He sold mixtapes through Stan's Square Records alongside the city's top DJs and is active on Instagram as @djdyce201, where the 201 area code confirms his Jersey City identity.

entries.name: DJ Q
facts: DJ Q — billed on records and flyers as DJ "Q" and known today as Da Topic — is a Jersey City mixtape and party DJ who came up through the city's late-1990s and 2000s club, mixtape, and battle circuit.
He was part of the Stan's Square Records mixtape generation, sharing bills and battle cards with peers including DJ 007, DJ Nel E Nel, DJ Dyce, DJ E Double, and DJ P Dub.

Flyers from the period place him across the era's marquee events — battling live at the 1st Annual Winter Blaze mixtape DJ competition in 2000, appearing on Laydown-N-Die Records' "Dream Team" poster, and billed at Summer Heatwave 2002 among "Jersey City's Finest."

He held club nights across the city and neighboring Newark — from Hipnotiq Thursdays at Foxes on St. Paul's Avenue to Stand Up Fridays at the Paradise Club on Broadway — and was among the invited DJs for Rap Wars Round 2 in 2003.
He is named in both DJ DX's and DJ 007's segments of the 2006 Jersey City DJ Documentary; his identity was confirmed by DJ DX in 2026.

entries.name: Strong Vic
facts: Strong Vic is a Jersey City DJ, producer, and beatmaker whose work runs through the heart of the city's mixtape era.

He is remembered above all for his mixtape intros — the custom drops and original beats that opened other DJs' tapes — a signature that made him one of the scene's most sought-after collaborators and left his fingerprint on a generation of Jersey City mixtapes.
A talented beatmaker, he built original production alongside his DJ work and is counted among the peers of DJ DX and the Stan's Square Records generation of city DJs.
He remains active in Jersey City under the handle @strongvic201, the 201 area code marking his place in the Hudson County DJ lineage.

He was identified and cataloged during the archive's effort to document the contemporary DJ community that keeps the city's venues and parties energized.
His work represents the ongoing continuum of Jersey City DJ culture, bridging the gap between historical mixtape eras and modern digital promotion.

entries.name: Joe Budden
facts: Joe Budden is a rapper and media personality raised in Jersey City from age 13, who moved from the city's mixtape circuit to a national profile and is one of the most commercially successful artists the city has produced.
His single 'Pump It Up' (2003) reached No. 38 on the Billboard Hot 100 and earned a Grammy nomination for Best Male Rap Solo Performance.

He is a founding member of the rap supergroup Slaughterhouse (with Royce da 5'9, Joell Ortiz, and Crooked I), signed to Eminem's Shady Records. Slaughterhouse's album Welcome to: Our House debuted at No. 2 on the Billboard 200 in 2012.
He has hosted The Joe Budden Podcast since 2015, surpassing 900 episodes and becoming one of hip-hop's most successful independent podcast platforms.

In the Chill Town J.C. Hip-Hop Documentary, older pioneers repeatedly invoke him as the city's most nationally visible rapper while debating why Chilltown's foundational scene never received wider recognition.

entries.name: Albee Al
facts: Albee Al (born Albert Robinson, 1988) is a rapper and songwriter from Jersey City's Marion Projects, known for gritty, street-level storytelling; his catalog has drawn millions of streams across Apple Music, Spotify, and YouTube.

He carries the stage name of his late father, Lord Sun Albee Al, founder of the pioneering Jersey City crew Sweet Slick and Slide — placing him in a direct line from one of Chilltown's foundational hip-hop families; DJ E Double and DJ Thunderkatt are his cousins.

After being acquitted in 2014 of a 2010 murder charge — for which he had spent roughly four years in jail awaiting trial — he sharply increased his musical output, releasing projects including Everyday June 30th, a tribute to his murdered brother, and Koba (2019), which peaked at No. 6 on the Billboard Heatseekers Albums chart.
He is affiliated with Flatline and Splashlyfe and is credited on DJ Swill B's 'Choppa in the Backseat' (2024); community voices count him among the city's current legends.

entries.name: Ransom
facts: Ransom (Randy Nicholls) is a Jersey City rapper who moved to the city around age eight and built his reputation as one of the most technically precise MCs on the New Jersey mixtape circuit.
He is one half of the A-Team alongside Hitchcock, a duo affiliated with DJ Clue's Desert Storm alongside Fabolous and Stack Bundles.
The A-Team released the Hardhood Classics mixtape trilogy, earning a reputation for punishing punchlines and grimy street verses that defined the era's bar-for-bar standard.
After a Def Jam deal did not materialize the A-Team split around 2006; Ransom has continued a prolific solo career.
In the A-Team era the duo was managed by Orlando Wharton — later a Capitol Music Group EVP and president of Priority Records — as part of his Presidential Crew.

He created DJ Kenny Kenn's Unsigned Hype DVD, which documented and promoted unsigned Jersey City talent during the DVD era -- a format that preceded YouTube as the primary way local artists reached audiences.
He produced the Promise Me Tomorrow documentary series and is remembered by peers as a staple of Jersey City culture who 'gave us all a shot to be on the tube way before social media.'

entries.name: Harold Adamson
facts: Harold Campbell Adamson (December 10, 1906 -- August 17, 1980) was an American lyricist born in Greenville, New Jersey, who became one of the most prolific popular songwriters of the mid-twentieth century.
He collaborated with composers including Jimmy McHugh, Burton Lane, and Hoagy Carmichael, writing lyrics for films, Broadway productions, and recordings across more than three decades.
His songs include 'Around the World', 'Everything I Have Is Yours', and 'Time on My Hands', featured in Hollywood productions from the 1930s through the 1960s.

entries.name: Robert "Kool" Bell
facts: Robert Earl 'Kool' Bell (born October 8, 1950), also known as Muhammad Bayyan, is an American musician, singer, and songwriter who grew up in Jersey City, New Jersey.
He co-founded Kool & the Gang in Jersey City in 1964 alongside his brother Ronald Bell and five neighborhood friends, all of whom attended Lincoln High School in the city.
As the band's bassist and a driving creative force, he helped build one of the most sampled catalogs in music history, spanning funk, soul, R&B, and pop across six decades.
The section of Maple Street in Jersey City where Bell and his bandmates grew up was officially renamed 'Kool & the Gang Way' in 2016 in their honor.

He co-founded the group in the mid-1960s alongside Robert 'Kool' Bell and other Jersey City neighbors, all of whom attended Lincoln High School, and served as the rhythmic anchor of the band for more than five decades.
As the group's primary drummer, he helped craft a sound blending jazz roots with hard funk and later crossover R&B, producing a catalog that is among the most sampled in music history.

entries.name: Brute Force
facts: Stephen Friedland (born September 29, 1940), known as Brute Force, is an American singer and songwriter from Jersey City, New Jersey, who was part of the New York avant-pop scene of the 1960s.

His work spanned jazz, country, rock, and pop, and his guitar playing is embedded in some of the most commercially successful recordings of the 1950s and 1960s.

entries.name: Kool & the Gang
facts: Kool & the Gang formed in Jersey City, New Jersey, in 1964, founded by brothers Robert 'Kool' Bell and Ronald Bell along with five neighborhood friends who all attended Lincoln High School in the city.
Before settling on their name the group went through earlier identities including the Jazziacs, the Soul Town Band, the New Dimensions, and Kool & the Flames.

They honed their sound in Jersey City basements, at St. John's Church, and at the Boys and Girls Club on Ash Street before achieving national and international success across funk, soul, R&B, and pop.
Their catalog is among the most sampled in music history, and Jersey City honored them in 2016 by officially renaming a section of Maple Street 'Kool & the Gang Way'.

Between 1969 and 1987 the group built one of the most enduring chart histories in R&B and pop, with 25 Top-10 R&B hits, 9 Top-10 pop hits, and 31 gold and platinum albums; worldwide album sales exceed 70 million.

Their 1970s street-funk era produced horn-driven anthems including “Jungle Boogie” (No. 4 pop, 1973) and “Hollywood Swinging” (No. 6 pop and their first No. 1 R&B single, 1974), after they first reached the Hot 100 in 1969 with the instrumental “Kool & the Gang”.

After adding lead singer James “J.T.” Taylor, the group reached its 1980s commercial peak with “Ladies' Night” (No. 8, 1979), “Too Hot” (No. 5, 1980), “Joanna” (No. 2, 1983), and “Cherish” (No. 2, 1985).
Their signature song “Celebration” spent two weeks at No. 1 on the Billboard Hot 100 in February 1981 and six weeks atop the R&B chart, and remains the group's only No. 1 pop hit.

entries.name: The Manhattans
facts: The Manhattans were an American soul and rhythm and blues vocal group formed in Jersey City, New Jersey, in 1962, and became one of the most successful vocal-harmony groups of the 1970s.

The original lineup paired lead tenor George “Smitty” Smith with bass singer and principal songwriter Winfred “Blue” Lovett, tenors Edward “Sonny” Bivins and Kenneth “Wally” Kelly, and baritone Richard Taylor.

Signed to Joe Evans's Carnival Records, the group reached the Billboard R&B chart with “I Wanna Be (Your Everything)” in 1965 and followed it with “Follow Your Heart” and “Baby I Need You,” building a devoted following on the soul and chitlin-circuit stages.

After the group moved to Deluxe Records, George Smith left in 1970 because of illness and died that December; Gerald Alston stepped in as lead singer, and in 1973 the group signed to Columbia Records.

On Columbia the Manhattans broke through nationally with “There's No Me Without You” (1973) before their signature hit: “Kiss and Say Goodbye,” written by Winfred Lovett and opened by his spoken monologue, reached No. 1 on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1976 and was among the first singles ever certified platinum by the RIAA.
“Shining Star” reached No. 5 on the Hot 100 in 1980 and won the Grammy Award for Best R&B Vocal Performance by a Duo, Group or Chorus.
Across their career the group charted 45 hits on the Billboard R&B chart, including twelve R&B top-10 singles, their close-harmony ballads carried by Alston's tenor lead and Lovett's bass narrations.

Richard Taylor left in the mid-1970s after converting to Islam, and Gerald Alston departed for a solo career in 1988, but Bivins and Lovett carried the name onward; the group's records remain staples of quiet-storm and oldies radio and a cornerstone of Jersey City's soul legacy.

entries.name: Chill Rob G
facts: Chill Rob G (born Robert Frazier) is a rapper from Jersey City, New Jersey, and a founding member of DJ Mark the 45 King's legendary Flavor Unit collective -- one of the defining hip-hop crews of the Golden Era.
His 1989 debut album 'Ride the Rhythm' (Wild Pitch Records), produced by The 45 King, is widely cited as a classic of the era, featuring 'Court Is Now in Session' and 'Dope Rhymes'.

His song 'Let the Words Flow' was sampled without permission by German dance group Snap! for their global hit 'The Power' (1990), one of the most commercially successful acts of unauthorized sampling in early 1990s pop.

He returned with 'Empires Crumble' (2022, SpitSLAM/Chuck D's label), a boom bap comeback featuring a Flavor Unit reunion with The 45 King, Lakim Shabazz, and others, followed by 'Survival of the Better' (2026), executive produced by Chuck D and produced entirely by C-Doc.

In the Chill Town J.C. Hip-Hop Documentary he speaks at length about the "Let the Words Flow" / "The Power" saga — recounting how Snap!'s Turbo B performed the reworked material while his own version stood on the smaller Wild Pitch label — and recalls the early Chilltown DJs who would not let him grab the microphone before his rise.

sources.label: Wikipedia — Chill Rob G

entries.name: DJ Wimpy Bee
facts: DJ Wimpy Bee (Edmund Bryant) was a Jersey City DJ legend celebrated as a founding figure of the Chilltown hip-hop scene and one of the most respected DJs in the city's history, known for technical mastery including super-fast scratches and deep command of the turntables.

He was named by DJ Flash as his 'big brother' in the craft -- the most direct form of mentorship credit in the scene -- placing Wimpy Bee at the top of the generational chain that built Jersey City's DJ tradition.

He was featured in documentaries chronicling the city's unsung hip-hop pioneers, including the Chill Town Jersey City documentary, and his influence spanned decades from early block parties and school events through club sets and recorded mixtapes.

Edmund Bryant passed away in October 2025. His death was met with an outpouring of respect from the Jersey City community, where he was remembered not only as a master of the turntables but as a 'special gentleman' deeply embedded in the city's cultural fabric.

In the Chill Town documentary, DJ after DJ names him their favorite; he recounts spinning nearly every venue in the city — his staple at P.S. 11 School, plus St. Patrick's, Sacred Heart, Foxes and the city's churches — and taking up the craft after a cousin, DJ Slick, told him he could not do it, starting out on borrowed Panasonics before moving to Numark turntables.
DJ Flash, in a firsthand account given to this archive in 2026, names him among the producers behind Double XX Posse's debut single "Executive Class" (Mel-O Records, 1990).

entries.name: Chill Divine
facts: Chill Divine, born Gary Van Liew, is a 1990s underground hip-hop artist known for a 12-inch EP originally released on Square Records in 1990

Square Records was founded by Stan Krause — the Jersey City record-store owner behind Stan's Square Records and Catamount Records — placing Chill Divine's debut within the Krause label lineage that runs from 1960s acappella and soul into the city's early hip-hop
The record pairs “Puttin’ It Wildly” and “Long Live the Lyrical”; the remastered four-track release adds “Puttin’ It Wildly (Dub)” and “Long Live the Lyrical (Instrumental)”
The music features boom-bap production and a classic lyrical style

A great-grandson of Sheldon Van Liew through Kenneth and Ardelle Van Liew, and the uncle of DJ DX (Robert Van Liew), Chill Divine embedded the Van Liew name in Jersey City's foundational hip-hop — one of four cornerstone figures in the family's six-decade musical legacy

entries.name: DJ Thurmie Thurm
facts: DJ Thurmie Thurm, also known as DJ Thurm, is a battle DJ and pioneer of the original Jersey City, New Jersey hip-hop and house scene, best known for his work in the late 1970s and early 1980s as a founding member of the Jersey City Chiba Crew.

He came up in the Chilltown scene -- Jersey City's nickname -- playing park jams, community centers, and local spots alongside legends including Grand Wizard Capone, Nicky Barnes, and the Sweet, Slick and Sly Crew.

He is featured in the documentary 'Chill Town J.C.', directed by A. Champagne Lloyd, which documents the hip-hop artists who developed their talents in the streets of Jersey City and places him alongside Chill Rob G, Lakim Shabazz, Master Cee, and DJ Wimpy Bee as a city pioneer.

Decades on, he remains a respected figure in Chilltown history, celebrated across East Coast music history circles and documented in community groups and online archives dedicated to Jersey City's founding era.

entries.name: Double J
facts: Double J (Gerald 'J' Jones), known as The Hitman Double J, is a Jersey City rapper and a founding member of Queen Latifah's original Flavor Unit collective, one of the defining hip-hop crews of the late 1980s and early 1990s.
Growing up in Jersey City he hosted block parties alongside future stars including Apache; in 1988 he joined DJ Mark the 45 King's Flavor Unit after an introduction through Apache and the crew.

He appears on the 1990 posse cut 'Flavor Unit Assassination Squad' (4th and Broadway/Island Records) and released the 12-inch 'Cannibal Town / Def Style' and solo album 'The Hitman', best known for the track 'Double J'z Tantrum'.
He later resurfaced as half of the Maniac Mob, reuniting with DJ Mark the 45 King for production on several independent releases including a track on the 1995 compilation 'The D&D Project'.

He received his Island Records deal after Fab Five Freddy saw a homemade video of the Flavor Unit crew and passed it up the chain -- a chain of events that shows how the early 1990s rap industry actually worked.

entries.name: DJ Stranger Dee
facts: DJ Stranger Dee is a legendary figure in the Jersey City hip-hop and DJ scene who gained local fame in the early 1980s as a foundational DJ and pioneer for the Sweet, Slick and Sly Crew -- one of the most celebrated acts in early New Jersey hip-hop.

Along with his crew, Stranger Dee performed at seminal events including New Jersey vs. Bronx showdowns at the Benmore Skating Rink in Jersey City; key documented dates include a January 15, 1982 showdown with the Cold Crush Brothers and the Turnout Battle of 82 on July 2 of that year, both preserved in flyers archived at the Museum of Pop Culture (MoPOP).

He is credited by DJ Flash -- who names Stranger D as an influence who let him spin as a child -- with being a founding presence in the park-jam scene that built Jersey City's DJ tradition from the ground up.

He made contributions on a national scale, working as a DJ on major hip-hop tours alongside groups including Public Enemy in the late 1980s, taking the Jersey City sound to a national touring circuit.
He is regarded by older generations and hip-hop historians as a founding father of the Chilltown (Jersey City) music movement.

Multiple artists in the Chill Town documentary name him the first DJ they ever heard in Jersey City — director Champagne recalls hearing him cutting break beats in Armstrong Park — underscoring his role as a founding presence in the city's park-jam scene.
DJ Flash, in a firsthand account given to this archive in 2026, credits him with supplying the instrument sample on Double XX Posse's debut single "Executive Class" (Mel-O Records, 1990).

entries.name: The Spellbinders
facts: The Spellbinders were a Jersey City soul and doo-wop group, formed in the mid-1960s by MacArthur Munford with James Wright, Bobby Shivers, Ben Grant, and Elouise Pennington

Signed to Columbia Records in 1965 under producer Van McCoy, they charted with the Top-25 R&B hit “For You” and “Help Me (Get Myself Back Together Again),” with “Chain Reaction” and “Help Me” hitting in the UK; their album The Magic of the Spellbinders is regarded as a soul classic
They toured extensively with Jackie Wilson, Joe Tex, and Wilson Pickett before breaking up by 1969

entries.name: MacArthur Munford
facts: MacArthur J. Munford (January 20, 1943 – September 5, 2006) was a Jersey City soul and doo-wop singer, the bass voice of The Spellbinders
Born into a working-class Jersey City family, he began in street-corner singing and joined his first group, the Valitons, by 1960 before co-founding the Spellbinders

In the fall of 1962, while singing with Mark Stevens and the Charmers, Munford was critically injured in an automobile accident on the Lincoln Highway Bridge (Route 1 & 9) heading toward Newark on a stormy night; fellow member Percy Fair and two young women, the Oliver sisters of Bayonne, were killed. The crash ended the group, and Munford spent a long time in critical condition before recovering to co-found the Spellbinders

Signed to Columbia Records in 1965 under producer Van McCoy, the Spellbinders charted with the Top-25 R&B hit “For You” and “Help Me (Get Myself Back Together Again)”; their album The Magic of the Spellbinders is considered a soul classic, and the group toured with Jackie Wilson, Joe Tex, and Wilson Pickett
He later formed the a cappella group Vintage with Ray Block of the Valitons, and died of cancer in 2006 at the age of 63

Through the Van Liew family, Munford is a cornerstone of a six-decade Jersey City musical dynasty: he is a grandson of Fredrick Van Liew — one of the twin brothers, with Sheldon Van Liew, who anchor the family — through his mother, Kathryn Van Liew

He is the maternal uncle of multi-platinum songwriter Mary Brown (daughter of his sister Lorraine H. Brown), and an elder of the line that reaches Chill Divine (Gary Van Liew) and DJ DX (Robert Van Liew) through Fredrick's twin, Sheldon

entries.name: Mary Brown
facts: Mary Brown is a Grammy-nominated singer, songwriter, and producer from Jersey City, New Jersey — among the most decorated writers of the modern R&B era, with 15× RIAA Platinum and 6× RIAA Gold certified credits, a two-time Grammy nominee best known for penning Destiny's Child's first Billboard No. 1, “No, No, No”
Operatically trained, she won the Governor's Award at the New Jersey Performing Arts Center competition as a teenager, earning a full scholarship to study music at Rutgers University

During her first year of college she signed to Reprise Records as one-third of Teddy Riley's girl-group trio Abstrac'; the group's single “Right and Hype” spent six weeks on the Billboard Hot 100 and reached No. 23 on the Hot Black Singles chart across a 17-week run
She went on to session and background work for artists including Brandy, Bobby Brown, Guy, and fellow New Jersey native Whitney Houston

Her songwriting credits include Destiny's Child's international No. 1 “No, No, No,” work for Jaheim, Day26's debut single “Got Me Goin’,” and Danity Kane's “Bad Girl” (featuring Missy Elliott) and “Ecstasy” (featuring Rick Ross) — the last two written on MTV's Making the Band 3, where Sean “Diddy” Combs praised her “mean pen game”

She contributed to Michael Jackson's final studio album, Invincible (2001), and has worked with Mary J. Blige, Wyclef Jean, Queen Latifah, Usher, SWV, Patti LaBelle, Diana Ross, and LL Cool J; her “No, No, No” was later sampled by Jack Harlow on his Grammy-nominated Come Home the Kids Miss You (2022)

Her honors include Soul Train's Lady of Soul Award and four BMI Publisher & Songwriter Awards; she co-chaired the Recording Academy's New York Songwriters & Composers Wing (2022–2023), runs the publishing company Ms. Mary's Music, and works as an educator and philanthropist

She is a granddaughter of Kathryn Van Liew and, with her brother Cool Sir Brown, one of the family's modern hitmakers; she is the niece of soul singer MacArthur Munford of the Spellbinders — her mother, Lorraine H. Brown, was Munford's sister — placing her among the cornerstone figures of the Van Liew musical dynasty alongside Chill Divine (Gary Van Liew) and DJ DX (Robert Van Liew)

By her account in the Chill Town J.C. Hip-Hop Documentary, her arranging gift showed early: as a teenager she wrote and arranged the backing harmonies that helped her brother Cool Sir Brown win an MC battle at P.S. 11, and her earliest studio work came through the family's friendship with Biz Markie and Doug E. Fresh.

entries.name: Catamount Records
facts: Catamount Records is a Jersey City record label founded in 1964 by Stan Krause, proprietor of the Stan's Square Records store
In its early years the label issued mostly doo-wop acappella records, later branching into soul, disco, and funk
Krause and Catamount helped launch the careers of groups including The Persuasions, 14 Karat Soul, the Royal Counts, Vintage, and Jo Ann & the Heartaches

Records were pressed in small runs — often as few as 25 and no more than 200 copies — including green, blue, red, and bronze colored vinyl now more sought-after than black pressings, made possible by Kay Records, the Little Ferry pressing plant owned by Krause's father
Skip Jackson, formerly of the Shantons, played a key production role at the label until his death in 1982

entries.name: The Persuasions
facts: The Persuasions are an American a cappella group long celebrated as “the kings of a cappella,” whose early career ran through Jersey City
Stan Krause discovered the group singing on the streets of Jersey City, recorded their first live tracks, and managed them in their early years

Frank Zappa first heard the group singing over the phone from Stan Krause's Stan's Square Records store; Krause managed the group and issued their early recordings on his Catamount Records label, after which they signed to a major label

The group charted on Billboard through the early 1970s: Street Corner Symphony (1972) reached No. 16 on the Billboard R&B Albums chart and No. 88 on the Billboard 200; We Came to Play (1971) reached No. 32 R&B; and Spread the Word (1972) reached No. 40 R&B
The group went on to a decades-long recording career across major and independent labels, influencing generations of a cappella and vocal-harmony singers

entries.name: Chill Town J.C. Hip-Hop Documentary
facts: The Chill Town J.C. Hip-Hop Documentary is a community-made film directed and edited by Champagne that gathers first-person interviews with the pioneers of Jersey City's hip-hop scene — the city long nicknamed "Chilltown."

It traces the culture from its early-1980s park-jam and block-party roots through the crews, DJs, and MCs who built the scene, including Sweet Slick and Slide, the Tranquilizing Three, and Positively Black, with the Chiba Crew and others in the mix.

On-camera voices include DJ Stranger Dee, DJ Wimpy Bee, Chill Rob G, DJ Nel E Nel, DJ 007, Prince Kyrie, and Cool Sir Brown, alongside visiting New York artists who frequented the city such as LA Sunshine of the Treacherous Three.

The film doubles as an argument for the city's place in hip-hop history, repeatedly returning to why Chilltown — despite its 1980s prominence — never received the national recognition its pioneers feel it earned.

sources.label: Chill Town J.C. Hip-Hop Documentary Film, dir. Champagne — YouTube

entries.name: Champagne
facts: Champagne, also known as Miss Champagne, is a Jersey City hip-hop pioneer — by the scene's account one of the city's first female MCs and artist managers — and the director and editor of the Chill Town J.C. Hip-Hop Documentary.
In the 1980s she was, for a time, a member of the Chiba (Cha) Crew, and the scene credits management of the conscious-rap group Positively Black to "Youth Champagne."
Decades later she convened many of Chilltown's surviving pioneers on camera, producing the documentary that preserves the scene's first-person history.

sources.label: Chill Town J.C. Hip-Hop Documentary Film, dir. Champagne — YouTube

entries.name: Sweet Slick and Slide
facts: Sweet Slick and Slide — also styled Sweet Slick and Sly and known as Triple S — was one of Jersey City's dominant hip-hop crews of the 1980s and early 1990s, founded by Lord Sun Albee Al.

By the accounts of peers, it was one of only two Chilltown crews — with the Tranquilizing Three — considered strong enough to battle the celebrated crews of New York, and it toured widely along the East Coast.

Its members included Jimmy Mack, Warren "Warm Daddy" Pray, the DJ Victor Quick, and Sugar Ray; founder Lord Sun Albee Al anchored a hip-hop family whose next generations include his son, the rapper Albee Al, and his nephews DJ E Double and DJ Thunderkatt.

entries.name: Lord Sun Albee Al
facts: Lord Sun Albee Al was the founder of the Jersey City crew Sweet Slick and Slide and one of the most revered party-rockers of the city's early hip-hop era.

Peers describe him as the man who could take over any party, from Jersey City to the Bronx, and credit him with drawing the respect of New York's crews to Chilltown; several artists name him as the reason they began rapping.
His musical family runs deep in the city: his son is the rapper Albee Al, and his nephews include DJ E Double and DJ Thunderkatt.
He is remembered throughout the documentary with the refrain "rest in peace" — a measure of his standing as a foundational figure of the scene.

entries.name: Positively Black
facts: Positively Black were a Jersey City conscious-rap group formed in 1988, built around a message of Black consciousness that set them apart from the city's other crews.

The group was led by Prince Kyrie with King N and the DJs Storm and Tyrell, and was managed by Youth Champagne, remembered as one of the city's first female managers and rappers; founding member Salam is remembered by the group, rest in peace.
They recorded for the family-run Trumpet Records before signing to Select Records, sharing a roster era with acts such as Chubb Rock, Kid 'n Play, and UTFO.

entries.name: DJ Count Basil

As a producer he is known for classic tracks such as “City Streets” and “Hardhaus II” on the influential Nu Groove label, and he is celebrated for his deep, classic, and soulful house sets.
He is also remembered in the Chill Town J.C. Hip-Hop Documentary as the DJ Basil who first put DJ Wimpy Bee on at a Lincoln High School party — an early node in the city’s DJ lineage.

entries.name: Orlando Wharton
facts: Orlando Wharton is a music executive who came up through Jersey City's early-2000s scene, beginning his career managing his close friends — the rappers Ransom and Hitchcock of the duo The A-Team.
Known in that era as "Orlando" or "Whartonberg," he built the Presidential Crew and its production arm, Presidential Beats, around the city's independent mixtape run.

After Ransom's incarceration and the death of affiliate Stack Bundles, Wharton stepped away from music before returning as an A&R executive — rising to Executive Vice President at Capitol Music Group and President of Priority Records.
As an A&R he is credited with discovering and signing a generation of stars, including Kodak Black, Fetty Wap, A Boogie Wit Da Hoodie, and XXXTentacion.

entries.name: The Catamounts
facts: The Catamounts were an influential soul and rhythm-and-blues ensemble from Jersey City, emerging during the mid-1970s regional music explosion and becoming a prominent representative of the “Jersey City Sound.”

Distinct from the strictly instrument-free, street-corner a cappella acts that preceded them, they bridged classic vocal harmony with the orchestration-backed, danceable deep soul that paved the way for early disco.
They recorded for Catamount Records, the Jersey City label owned by Stan Krause, who also owned the group.
Decades later the group is featured on “Blessings” by DJ DX, carrying the Catamount sound into a new generation of the city's music.

entries.name: Money Train Entertainment
facts: Money Train Entertainment is a Jersey City media, event-production, and artist-management collective founded by entrepreneur and marketing executive Shakim Thompson.

Rooted in the nightlife culture of Jersey City and the wider North Jersey and New York metropolitan area, the company built a reputation for dominating regional street promotion, growing from high-energy parties across the city into a full-scale entertainment network.
Money Train hosted the Winter Blaze DJ Battle — the area’s first premier seasonal turntablist competition — pitting elite regional DJs against each other live.

Thompson expanded the brand into media with a music-and-lifestyle television show on Channel 51 (Comcast) reaching some 2.5 million residents; filmed and directed by WizTV, it featured major recording artists while spotlighting the turntablism of DJ Wizard and the blends of DJ DX, bridging street culture and mainstream television.

entries.name: Soul Shades
facts: Soul Shades is a duo built around Jersey City’s DJ DX — who handles turntablism, rap, and production — alongside multi-instrumentalist Julie Schatz on piano and violin, the two trading between the decks and live instrumentation.

Their breakout — a mashup of Nimino’s “I Only Smoke When I Drink” with Sting’s “Englishman in New York” — drew more than 10 million views across TikTok and Instagram, including 5 million on Ninja Tune’s own TikTok, and was publicly co-signed by Nimino himself; Ninja Tune and its Counter Records imprint posted it in late 2024, crediting Soul Shades.
The duo went on to further success with their release “Buzz In London.”

entries.name: The A-Team
facts: The A-Team were an early-2000s Jersey City rap duo of Ransom and Hitchcock, celebrated for their punchline-heavy, grimy street lyricism.

Managed by Orlando Wharton and affiliated with DJ Clue's Desert Storm, they built a strong underground following through their Hardhood Classics mixtape series and ties to artists such as Joe Budden and Stack Bundles.
After a major-label deal did not materialize the duo split around 2006; Ransom went on to a prolific solo career and Wharton to a landmark executive run.

entries.name: Zigimo
facts: Zigimo is an American multi-instrumentalist, producer, and recording artist from Jersey City, New Jersey, working in experimental hip-hop, underground rap, and lo-fi instrumental production.

A graduate of the Jersey City Arts Program at Henry Snyder High School, he trained on tuba and piano — the tuba shaping his low-end and bassline approach, and the piano serving as the harmonic basis of his songwriting.
His production centers on texture-heavy beats, distortion, and plugg-adjacent lo-fi digital styles, joining conservatory training to internet-native underground forms.

He maintains a prolific catalog of albums, mixtapes, and singles on platforms including Spotify and Bandcamp, among them the projects imo, DELTA.77, UNRELEASED PROJECTS, and COLLECTION OF INSTRUMENTALS FROM ZIGIMO (2018–2021).
His singles and EPs include “couldn't refresh,” “EYE FTM,” “network.1,” “formula,” “bandit/mindset!,” “Testing Code Z,” “SIGNAL.1,” and “banjax.”
He is a son of Jersey City DJ and producer DJ DX (Robert Van Liew), placing him in the next generation of the city's musical lineage.

entries.name: Roy Hamilton
facts: Roy Hamilton was raised in Jersey City from his early teens, after his family moved from Leesburg, Georgia around 1943, and he was discovered performing in the city.
He sang in Jersey City church choirs with the Searchlight Gospel Singers and boxed Golden Gloves before signing with Epic Records, where he became the label's first star.
His recording of “You'll Never Walk Alone” spent eight weeks at number 1 on the Billboard R&B chart in 1954.
“Unchained Melody” reached number 1 R&B and the pop top 10 in 1955, and “Don't Let Go” reached number 13 pop in 1958, a record often cited as the first rock and roll hit recorded in stereo.
“You Can Have Her” reached number 12 pop in 1961. Hamilton was a documented influence on Elvis Presley.
He died in 1969 at the age of 40.

By family account, given to this archive in 2026 by DJ Flash, Hamilton's line runs into the city's hip-hop era: DJ Flash, who records as DJ Flash Hamilton and is credited with originating Jersey City's mixtape scene, and the late rapper Rigga Mortis, first cousins, name him as their great-great-grandfather.

entries.name: Frank Infante
facts: Frank Infante was born in Jersey City on November 15, 1951, and became the guitarist of Blondie through the band's commercial peak.
As a member of Blondie he played on the albums Parallel Lines, Eat to the Beat, and Autoamerican.
Those records carried four Billboard Hot 100 number 1 singles, credited to the band: “Heart of Glass” (1979), “Call Me” (1980), “The Tide Is High” (1980), and “Rapture” (1981).
He was inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame as a member of Blondie in 2006.

entries.name: Ozzie Nelson
facts: Ozzie Nelson was born in Jersey City on March 20, 1906, and became one of the most popular American bandleaders of the 1930s.

His orchestra recorded a string of national hits in the 1930s, including “And Then Some,” reported as a number 1 record in 1935 in the era before the Billboard singles chart existed (retrospective ranking per Joel Whitburn's Pop Memories).
He later created and starred in the radio and television series The Adventures of Ozzie and Harriet, and was the father of the rock and roll singer Ricky Nelson.

entries.name: Flip Wilson
facts: Flip Wilson, born Clerow Wilson Jr. in Jersey City on December 8, 1933, grew up in the city and became one of the most popular American comedians of the early 1970s.
His comedy album The Devil Made Me Buy This Dress won the 1971 Grammy Award for Best Comedy Recording.
His comedy albums charted on the Billboard 200, an unusual crossover for a spoken word act. (The exact peak position is pending source confirmation before it is printed.)
He hosted The Flip Wilson Show on NBC from 1970 to 1974, one of the highest rated programs of its era.

entries.name: Stan Krause
facts: Stan Krause was the Jersey City record store owner, record producer, and label founder behind Stan's Square Records and Catamount Records, a central figure in the city's acappella and rhythm-and-blues history.
He founded Catamount Records in 1964 and opened Stan's Square Records in 1965, and the label launched the recording careers of vocal groups including The Persuasions and 14 Karat Soul.
Frank Zappa first heard The Persuasions singing over the phone from Krause's store, where Krause managed the group and recorded their first live tracks.

Krause's acts reached the national stage: The Persuasions charted on Billboard (Street Corner Symphony, No. 16 R&B and No. 88 pop, 1972), and 14 Karat Soul performed on Saturday Night Live in 1983 and drew large audiences in Japan.
He founded the United in Group Harmony Association (UGHA) and produced live vocal-harmony concerts for decades under the banner Classic Harmony Productions.
In 2015, Jersey City Mayor Steven Fulop honored him with a proclamation as “Stan the Music Man of Jersey City,” and in 2016 he received the Jersey City Living Legend Award.
Krause died on May 20, 2016, at the age of 74, and his store closed in 2018.

entries.name: Skip Jackson
facts: Skip Jackson — born Tommy Brown — was a Jersey City singer, arranger, and producer who wrote and produced much of the catalog of Stan Krause's Catamount Records.

He came up in the doo-wop era with the Shantons, neighborhood friends from Jersey City who began singing together in basements and at local dances; by his biographer's account he learned piano as a boy on a discarded instrument in the coal bin where his father worked as a building manager.

Through the 1970s he was the musical hand behind much of Catamount's output, arranging and producing the label's sides and, on some records, supplying the instrumentation and most of the vocals himself.
Under his own name he recorded as Skip Jackson & the Natural Experience, whose “Micro Wave Boogie” (Catamount, 1979) headlined the label's disco-era series.

His Catamount arrangement and production credits include 14 Karat Soul's “Doo-Wopp Disco” and “Please Say You Want Me,” the Catamounts' “Creation of Love,” the Metro-Liners, the Bonaires, and Greenville's “Just Got To Party.”
He continued making records with Stan Krause until his death in 1982.

entries.name: TGK
facts: TGK were a mid-1990s hip-hop group whose known release, the single and EP “Avenue Ride,” came out in 1995 on Catamount Records — Stan Krause's Jersey City label — in a co-release with Trumpet Records.
Issued on a 12-inch vinyl and a CD single dated November 4, 1995, the record collected several mixes of the song, among them a Radio Version, a Long Street Version, and the Spider D Club Mix.

The release places the group in the Jersey City lineage of Catamount and Trumpet, the small local labels through which much of the city's street-level talent was issued; biographical detail on the individual members is scarce and is not printed here pending a primary source.

entries.name: Wilson El Divino
facts: Wilson El Divino is a Jersey City recording artist, songwriter, and brand strategist working in the tristate area's Latin urban music scene.
Of Dominican and Puerto Rican descent, he draws on his Afro-Caribbean background throughout his music and worldview.

He describes his sound as “Afroton del Norte,” a blend of reggaeton, Afroton, and Afro-Caribbean soul that carries conscious, socially aware lyrics rather than standard party tropes, and releases music under the slogan “MUNDIAL, pero no para todo el MUNDO” (Global, but not for everyone).

Rooted in the Northeast mixtape circuit, he has collaborated and promoted alongside Jersey City figures including DJ DX and DJ Madden; his tracks include “Gone (Spanglish),” “Diosa,” “Complicada,” and “Futura Ex.”
He is a cousin of the late artist Liqui Loco, who appeared on regional urban tracks such as the “Dominicans Bang Bang (Remix)” alongside Juelz Santana, DJ DX, and J.R. Writer.
Outside of music, he is a creative strategist and the founder of the independent cultural brand and platform Cave of the Underworld.

entries.name: Mixed Company
facts: Mixed Company was an a cappella and street-corner vocal quintet formed in 1982 on Henry Street in Jersey City, blending doo-wop, Motown, rock, and rhythm and blues.

The group came together after the breakup of the Jersey City street-corner outfit the Street-Tones (later Patty and the Street-Tones), and took its name to reflect its mix of nationalities, genders, and personalities.

The 1982 lineup was Patty Zielinski, John “Bull” Zielinski, Jimmy Woods, Jeffrey Chambers, and Charles “Cada” Brooks on bass vocals; they sang together until 1993, when they added instrumentation and became Slick and Soul.
For Jersey City's Catamount Records they cut a now sought-after 45, a cover of Leiber and Stoller's “Kansas City” backed with Barbara Mason's “Yes, I'm Ready.”
Their album Buzzin' With Mixed Company paired classic doo-wop with country and down-home R&B, and their catalog was later reissued in officially remastered digital form.
Performing over the decades as The Original Mixed Company out of nearby Bayonne, members shared stages with national oldies acts including The Drifters, The Marvelettes, and The Platters.

entries.name: Mr. Low Kash 'N Da Shady Bunch
facts: Mr. Low Kash 'N Da Shady Bunch were a Jersey City hip-hop act of the mid-1990s, part of the city's boom-bap rap generation.
Their album Forever Raw was originally issued in 1996; its catalog survives on streaming, with tracks including “Recognize The Low,” “Heatholder's,” “The Best At This,” and “Down With Me.”
In 2022 Forever Raw was reissued on vinyl for the first time by Most Wanted Records as a numbered, limited double LP, the listing tagging the group's origin as Jersey City, New Jersey.

entries.name: CF201 (Constant Flow)
facts: CF — Constant Flow, who records as CF201 — is a Jersey City rapper who has worked the underground hip-hop scene since 1993, coming up through freestyle battles in his hometown before joining the New York open-mic circuit at Washington Square Park cyphers and rooms like the Nuyorican Poets Café and End of the Weak.

He has shared stages with Akon, Mobb Deep, and Immortal Technique, released his first official mixtape, Storm Mode, on Creative Juices Music in 2011, and has been counted among the Viper Records family.

His 2026 album Pizza Warz is, in his words, “a love letter to Monmouth Street in Downtown Jersey City” and the old Italian neighborhood he grew up in — built around the real price war between two Monmouth Street pizzerias — produced by Soulful Soze, mixed by SpeedyBabyy, and carrying a tribute to the late rapper Brav Divi.

entries.name: The Vocal-Teens
facts: The Vocal-Teens were a Jersey City doo-wop group of the late 1950s, formed while its members were still in high school.

Their signature side, “Be A Slave,” was written and sung by Michael Dennis Kelly at age fourteen; the group cut it with “Till Then” as demo material in 1958–59, and the two sides sat unreleased until 1972, when Downstairs Records issued them as a 45 (Downstairs 1000).
The record became a sought-after collector's piece among doo-wop enthusiasts, trading hands at auction decades later and circulating online as an example of uptempo Jersey City doo-wop.

A group photograph preserved by the late Mike Kelly and surfaced through the doo-wop collector community names the lineup as Mike Kelly (lead on “Be A Slave”), Henry Mays (lead on the flip side), Frederick Worrell, Junior Brooks, and Sonny Campbell.
The group opened the door for Kelly's professional career: he went on to join fellow Jersey City group The Duprees, becoming a permanent lead singer in 1964.

Family testimony corroborates the lineup: Errol Mallory — nephew of member Frederick Worrell and named after him — confirmed in a 2026 collector-community thread that “Freddy Errol Worrell” was his father's brother, placing the Worrell family in the heart of Jersey City's 1950s–60s vocal-group scene.

entries.name: Double XX Posse
facts: Double XX Posse were a Jersey City rap group of the early 1990s. The trio was billed as Sugar Ray, Peter Barnes, and DJ BK.

Their debut single, "Executive Class" backed with "On A Mission," came out in 1990 on Jersey City's Mel-O Records, with label production credits to Mel-O Odoms and Ray Howell; Big Beat reissued it in 1991 and signed the group.

By the firsthand account of DJ Flash, a co-producer on the session (given to this archive in 2026), "Executive Class" was built by a room of Jersey City DJs: Flash on the drums, DJ Stranger Dee supplying the instrument sample, brothers Sugar Chris and Magic creating the bassline, and DJ Wimpy Bee also producing.

Their 1992 single "Not Gonna Be Able to Do It" reached No. 1 on the Billboard Hot Rap Singles chart, and the follow-up "Head Cracker" peaked at No. 15; the hit also appeared in a season 3 episode of The Fresh Prince of Bel-Air.
They released the album Put Ya Boots On in 1992 on Big Beat, an Atlantic subsidiary, and Ruff, Rugged and Raw in 1995 on Atlantic, which carried the Lord Finesse produced single "Money Talks."

The group's name traveled with the city's own: the 1995 Usenet Rap Dictionary defined "Chilltown" as "Jersey City, Double XX Posse," and Jersey City DJ and archive founder DJ DX took his name from the group.

entries.name: Mel Odoms
facts: Mel Odoms, born Melvin Odoms, was a producer, songwriter, and founder of the Jersey City label Mel-O Records, active in the city's music scene of the 1980s and early 1990s.

Per his Discogs artist profile, he was a college track star and a sometime contract employee for Kool & the Gang, and he worked with many Jersey City musicians to get the funk and soul group Tomorrow's Edition their first records released, including the single "U Turn Me On" (CBS, 1981) and the album A Song for Everyone (Atlantic, 1982), produced by Amir Bayyan of the Kool & the Gang family.

He founded the independent label Mel-O Records, which in 1990 released the debut single of Jersey City rap group Double XX Posse, "Executive Class" backed with "On A Mission," carrying his production credit as Mel-O Odoms.
His Discogs profile, citing newspaper coverage, records that he was shot by a police officer and received a substantial financial award as a result.

entries.name: Mel-O Records
facts: Mel-O Records was an independent Jersey City record label founded by producer Mel Odoms; its Discogs label page lists its contact as Jersey City, NJ, and states it was founded to release Tomorrow's Edition records.
The label pressed its address onto its records: 94 Union Street, Jersey City, NJ 07304, with a 201 phone number, placing its operation in the city's Bergen-Lafayette section.

Its catalog ran from 1981 to 1990: Tomorrow's Edition's "U Turn Me On" (MLO-437, 1981), "I'm The Best" (MLO-438, 1984), and "Million Dollars" (MLO 800 and MLO 900, 1987), plus "Things Ain't As Bad As They Seem" (MLO 777, 1985), a promo 12-inch by an act billed simply as Jersey, written by K. Nance and D. Sanchez and produced by Amir Bayyan, Adam Bayyan, and Dave Dastard for Mel-O Productions, with Melvin Odoms as executive producer.

Its final and best-known release was the debut single of Jersey City rap group Double XX Posse, "Executive Class" backed with "On A Mission" (MLO-1100, 1990), with production credited to Mel-O Odoms and Ray Howell; Big Beat reissued it in 1991 and signed the group, who reached No. 1 on Billboard's Hot Rap Singles in 1992.

By the firsthand account of DJ Flash, given to this archive in 2026, the "Executive Class" session drew on a room of Jersey City DJs, including Flash on drums, DJ Stranger Dee on the sample, DJ Wimpy Bee producing, and brothers Sugar Chris and Magic on the bassline.

entries.name: Tomorrow's Edition
facts: Tomorrow's Edition were a funk and soul vocal group of the Jersey City scene of the 1980s; the Jersey City label Mel-O Records was founded by producer Mel Odoms specifically to release their records.

Their run opened with the single "U Turn Me On" (Mel-O MLO-437, 1981, picked up by CBS) and peaked with the album A Song for Everyone (Atlantic 80009-1, 1982), produced by Amir Bayyan of the Kool & the Gang family and remembered by collectors as one of the strongest lesser-known major-label soul albums of its era.
They continued on Mel-O through the 1980s with "I'm The Best" (1984) and "Million Dollars" (1987), and sang background vocals on Kool & the Gang recordings.

names.name: Jersey (act on Mel-O, 1985)
names.note: Billed simply as 'Jersey': Things Ain't As Bad As They Seem, 12-inch promo, Mel-O MLO 777, 1985. Label credits: written by K. Nance and D. Sanchez; produced by Amir Bayyan, Adam Bayyan, and Dave Dastard for Mel-O Productions; exec. producer Melvin Odoms. Nance and Sanchez may be the members. Ask Robert / the Odoms circle who the act was.
